title: Topic 18: The Honeypot Strategy: How Game Companies Use Fake Cheats to Catch Cheaters
content:
### Abstract

In the hallowed, pixelated halls of online gaming, where glory is sought and digital dynasties are forged, a clandestine war is perpetually waged. This is not a war of factions or guilds, but a far more insidious conflict between the very gods of these digital realms—the game developers—and a shadowy cabal of their own creation: the cheaters. This paper delves into one of the most cunning and delightfully ironic tactics deployed in this ongoing conflict—the “honeypot” strategy. We explore how game developers, with a flair for the dramatic and a deep understanding of their quarry, construct elaborate traps disguised as illicit cheats. These digital Trojan horses, far from offering an unfair advantage, serve as a one-way ticket to the digital gallows, a permanent ban from the game. Through a synthesis of case studies, psychological theory, and a healthy dose of schadenfreude, this paper examines the honeypot strategy as a masterclass in psychological warfare, a testament to the old adage that to catch a thief, one must think like a thief, and occasionally, offer them a shiny, booby-trapped apple.

### Introduction

The desire to win, to be the last one standing, the digital top dog, is a powerful motivator. It’s what drives players to spend countless hours honing their skills, memorizing maps, and perfecting their virtual headshots. But for some, the path to victory is not paved with dedication and practice, but with the digital equivalent of performance-enhancing drugs: cheats. From aimbots that grant preternatural accuracy to wallhacks that reveal the hidden locations of opponents, the world of online gaming is rife with those who would rather download a victory than earn it. This has led to an arms race of epic proportions, a digital Cold War between cheat developers and the anti-cheat software designed to stop them.

For years, the anti-cheat playbook was largely defensive, a digital Maginot Line of code designed to detect and block unauthorized software. But as cheaters grew more sophisticated, so too did the need for a more proactive, and dare we say, more entertaining approach. Enter the honeypot strategy, a method as old as espionage itself, repurposed for the digital age. The concept is simple yet brilliant: create a fake cheat, a digital siren song so alluring that only the most morally flexible of players can resist its call. This isn’t just about catching cheaters; it’s about a delicious, karmic justice. It’s about watching the hubris of a cheater turn to ash as they realize the very tool they thought would guarantee their victory was, in fact, their undoing. This paper will explore the intricacies of this strategy, from its technical implementation to the psychological vulnerabilities it exploits, and celebrate it for what it is: a truly elegant weapon for a more civilized age of online gaming.

### Literature Review

The ivory towers of academia, often pictured as serene sanctuaries of leather-bound books and contemplative silence, have, surprisingly, not remained entirely deaf to the cacophonous symphony of online gaming. A burgeoning field of inquiry has cautiously dipped its toes into the murky waters of digital deviancy, seeking to understand the motivations of those who choose the low road to victory. A growing body of research has begun to explore the whys and wherefores of cheating in the virtual realm. Much of this research, however, has focused on the cheater’s motivations, a sort of digital psychoanalysis of the morally bankrupt. Studies such as "Why Do Some Users Become Enticed to Cheating in Competitive Online Games?" by Jeong et al. (2021) have delved into the psychological cocktail of competitive motivation, self-esteem, and aggression that can lead a player down the dark path of digital deception. The study found that a high degree of competitive motivation, coupled with aggression, is a strong predictor of cheating behavior, while high self-esteem acts as a deterrent. In essence, those who are desperate to win and prone to frustration are more likely to cheat, a finding that will surprise approximately no one who has ever been on the receiving end of a 12-year-old’s expletive-laden tirade in a Call of Duty lobby.

While the psychology of the cheater is a fascinating, if somewhat depressing, field of study, the literature on anti-cheating strategies is more varied. Traditionally, the focus has been on technological solutions: client-side detection, server-side analysis, and the endless cat-and-mouse game of patching vulnerabilities. However, the concept of the honeypot, while a staple in the broader field of cybersecurity, has only recently begun to receive attention in the context of online gaming. In cybersecurity, a honeypot is a decoy system designed to lure in attackers, allowing security professionals to study their methods and gather intelligence. As noted by countless cybersecurity experts, the goal is not just to catch the intruder, but to understand them. This same principle applies to the gaming world, where a well-placed honeypot can not only ensnare a large number of cheaters but also provide valuable data on the types of cheats being used and the communities that develop and distribute them. The beauty of the honeypot strategy is that it shifts the paradigm from a defensive to an offensive posture. It’s a digital sting operation, and as we will see, it can be devastatingly effective.

### Analysis

The effectiveness of the honeypot strategy lies in its exploitation of fundamental human psychological principles. At its core, the strategy is a masterclass in temptation and consequence, a digital morality play where the cheater is both the protagonist and the architect of their own downfall. The first psychological pillar upon which the honeypot rests is the concept of **cognitive dissonance**. This theory, first proposed by Leon Festinger, posits that individuals experience mental discomfort when their beliefs or behaviors are in conflict with one another. In the context of cheating, a player may believe that they are a skilled gamer, while simultaneously knowing that they are using unfair advantages. To resolve this dissonance, they may rationalize their behavior, telling themselves that “everyone else is doing it” or that the game itself is unfair. The honeypot preys on this rationalization. By presenting a seemingly easy and consequence-free way to cheat, it lowers the barrier to entry, making it easier for players to succumb to temptation and resolve their cognitive dissonance in favor of cheating.

The second, and perhaps most deliciously ironic, psychological principle at play is the **illusion of control**. Humans have an innate desire to feel in control of their environment and their outcomes. Cheating, in a sense, is the ultimate expression of this desire. It allows players to bend the rules of the game to their will, to guarantee a victory that might otherwise be uncertain. The honeypot exploits this by offering a false sense of control. The cheater believes they are the one in power, the one with the secret weapon. In reality, they are a puppet, their every move monitored and recorded. The moment of realization, when the ban hammer falls and the illusion of control is shattered, is a powerful psychological blow.

Finally, the honeypot strategy leverages the power of **social proof**. In ambiguous social situations, people often look to others to determine the appropriate way to behave. In the world of online gaming, where cheating is often perceived as rampant, a player may be more inclined to cheat if they believe that many others are doing so. The creators of fake cheats often use this to their advantage, creating fake communities and testimonials to make their wares seem legitimate and widely used. This creates a feedback loop: the more people who fall for the honeypot, the more “social proof” there is that the cheat is real, and the more people are likely to fall for it in the future. It’s a digital pyramid scheme of deception, and the only ones who profit are the game developers.

### Real-World Examples

Perhaps the most famous and well-documented example of the honeypot strategy in action comes from Valve, the creators of the massively popular online game *Dota 2*. In early 2023, Valve announced that it had permanently banned over 40,000 accounts for cheating. The method they used was a textbook honeypot. Valve had identified a third-party cheat that was allowing players to access information that should have been hidden from them. Rather than simply patching the vulnerability, Valve decided to set a trap. They released a patch that created a “secret” area of data within the game client. This data was completely inaccessible during normal gameplay. The only way to access it was by using the specific cheat they were targeting.

The result was a digital dragnet of epic proportions. Every single account that accessed this “secret” area was flagged, providing Valve with what they described as “extremely high confidence that every ban was well-deserved.” The beauty of this approach was its surgical precision. There were no false positives, no innocent bystanders caught in the crossfire. The only people who were banned were those who had actively sought out and used the cheat. The public announcement of the ban wave served as a powerful deterrent, a clear message to the community that cheating would not be tolerated. It was a masterstroke of both technical and psychological warfare, and a clear demonstration of the power of the honeypot strategy.

While the *Dota 2* case is the most high-profile example, it is by no means the only one. Other game developers, not to be outdone in the art of digital subterfuge, have employed similar tactics, often with a delightful sense of humor. The annals of gaming lore are filled with tales of so-called “troll cheats.” These are not your garden-variety honeypots designed for quiet capture; they are instruments of public humiliation. Imagine, if you will, a cheater in a high-stakes firefight, suddenly finding their character inexplicably breaking into a flamboyant dance routine, or their weapon transformed into a harmless rubber chicken. One particularly notorious example comes from the developers of *PlayerUnknown's Battlegrounds (PUBG)*, who have been known to implement 'karma-based' punishments, where cheaters might find their grenades inexplicably exploding in their own hands. These troll cheats are a form of digital schadenfreude, a public spectacle that serves as both a punishment and a warning. These examples highlight the versatility of the honeypot strategy. It can be a tool for mass-banning, a surgical strike against a specific cheat, or a source of endless amusement for the non-cheating majority.

### Discussion

The honeypot strategy, while undeniably effective and entertaining, also raises some interesting questions about the nature of trust and deception in the digital age. In a world where our interactions are increasingly mediated by technology, how do we know who or what to trust? The honeypot strategy is a stark reminder that not everything is as it seems, and that even the most alluring of offers can be a trap. This has implications that extend far beyond the world of online gaming. The same psychological principles that make the honeypot strategy so effective in catching cheaters are also used by malicious actors in the real world. Phishing scams, for example, are essentially a form of honeypot, luring in victims with the promise of a prize or the threat of a penalty.

From a consumer behavior perspective, the honeypot strategy highlights the power of temptation and the human desire for a shortcut. We live in a culture of instant gratification, where we are constantly bombarded with messages that promise quick and easy solutions to our problems. The cheater, in this sense, is the ultimate consumer, willing to take a risk on a product that promises to deliver victory with minimal effort. The honeypot strategy, in this light, can be seen as a brilliant subversion of consumer culture. It is a product designed to fail, a service that offers only damnation. It is a masterclass in anti-marketing, using the very language of instant gratification and effortless success to ensnare its target demographic. It’s a form of marketing jujitsu, turning the cheater's own consumerist desires into the very instrument of their demise.

Ultimately, the honeypot strategy is a reflection of the ongoing co-evolution of technology and human behavior. As our digital worlds become more complex and immersive, so too will the methods used to both exploit and protect them. The battle between cheaters and game developers is a microcosm of a larger struggle, a struggle to define the rules of engagement in a world where the lines between the real and the virtual are increasingly blurred. The honeypot strategy is a reminder that in this new world, the most effective weapons are not always the most powerful, but the most clever.

### Conclusion

The war against cheating in online games is a war that will likely never be won. As long as there is a desire to win, there will be those who are willing to cheat to achieve it. But as the honeypot strategy so brilliantly demonstrates, the good guys are getting smarter. They are no longer content to simply build higher walls; they are now setting traps, laying snares, and using the cheater’s own psychology against them. The honeypot is more than just a clever piece of code; it’s a statement. It’s a declaration that the game is not just about who has the fastest reflexes or the most powerful hardware, but also about who is the most cunning.

So, to all the would-be cheaters out there, a word of advice: the next time you are tempted by the siren song of a new and improved cheat, take a moment to consider the possibility that you are not the hunter, but the prey. The digital cheese in this exquisitely crafted mousetrap may glitter with the promise of ill-gotten glory, but the snap of the ban hammer is both swift and merciless. And for the rest of us, the noble, the dedicated, the players who believe in the sanctity of a fair fight, there is a quiet satisfaction in knowing that somewhere in the world, a game developer is leaning back in their chair, a wry smile playing on their lips, as they watch another unsuspecting cheater march gleefully into their digital oubliette.
references:
[1] Jeong, E. J., Lee, D. Y., & Kim, G. M. (2021). Why Do Some Users Become Enticed to Cheating in Competitive Online Games? An Empirical Study of Cheating Focused on Competitive Motivation, Self-Esteem, and Aggression. *Frontiers in Psychology*, *12*, 768825. https://doi.org/10.3389/fpsyg.2021.768825

[2] Kerr, C. (2023, February 23). *Valve uses “honeypot” to catch and permanently ban 40,000 cheaters in Dota*. Game Developer. https://www.gamedeveloper.com/business/valve-uses-honeypot-to-catch-and-permanently-ban-40-000-cheaters-in-dota
